summary: Matt Rocklin explores whether dask can be extended to support out-of-core DataFrames, similar to what dask.array achieved for NumPy. He explains that a naive approach of partitioning by block size only supports limited operations, while partitioning by index values enables joins, complex groupby operations, and rolling windows. The key challenge is re-indexing and shuffling data across partitions, which requires finding good partition values and efficiently moving data between blocks. He presents a working but slow implementation and candidly admits the approach has significant limitations including forced evaluation during operations, slow shuffling, and single-core constraints due to Python's GIL.
key_points: Partitioning DataFrames by index values rather than block size enables complex operations like joins, groupby-apply, and rolling windows
Complex operations become easy when the logic aligns with the index, leading to a two-step pattern: re-index then compute
Re-indexing out-of-core data requires external sorting to find partition values, then shuffling data across all blocks
Unlike dask.array, dask.frame requires evaluating the computation graph during set_index operations rather than deferring all execution
The implementation works but faces significant performance challenges from DataFrame serialization, sorting complexity, and GIL limitations
money_quotes: Can we represent a large DataFrame as a sequence of in-memory DataFrames and perform most Pandas operations using task scheduling? I don't know. Lets try.
Complex operations are easy if the logic aligns with the index
Re-indexing and shuffling large data is difficult and expensive.
We can't just call DataFrame.sort because the entire data might not fit in memory and most of our sorting algorithms assume random access.
How feasible is it to frequently re-index a large amount of data? When do we reach the stage of 'just use a database'?
I know less about tables than about arrays. I'm ignorant of the literature and common solutions in this field. If anything here looks suspicious then please speak up.
key_insight: Building out-of-core DataFrames is fundamentally harder than out-of-core arrays because complex tabular operations require data to be partitioned by index values, necessitating expensive shuffle operations that force eager evaluation.
